patches.rationale: Arithmetic error: e^(−1.74) = 0.17552, so 8537 × 0.17552 = 1,498 km, not 1,270 km. Verified by curmudgeon Cycle 2.

patches.find: Using h = 8,537 km (pole) gives f = 300,000 / (4 × 8,537) ≈ 8.77 Hz. Using h = 1,270 km (equator) gives f = 300,000 / (4 × 1,270) ≈ 59 Hz. Neither matches the observed 7.83 Hz. The author avoids this by not specifying which height to use and by silently switching to the globe formula f ≈ c / (2 × π × R_sphere) ≈ 7.83 Hz. But that formula assumes a sphere, not his dome cavity. The dome's own geometry predicts ~22 Hz as a best estimate (averaging pole and equatorial heights), contradicting both the observed 7.83 Hz and the author's claim. He resolves the contradiction by abandoning his geometry and using the globe formula.
patches.replace: Using h = 8,537 km (pole) gives f = 300,000 / (4 × 8,537) ≈ 8.77 Hz. Using h = 1,498 km (equator, r = 15,000 km) gives f = 300,000 / (4 × 1,498) ≈ 50 Hz. Neither matches the observed 7.83 Hz. The author avoids this by not specifying which height to use and by silently switching to the globe formula f ≈ c / (2 × π × R_sphere) ≈ 7.83 Hz. But that formula assumes a sphere, not his dome cavity. A rigorous radial average gives a defensible best estimate: the linear radial average H_eff = H₀·(L/R)·(1 − exp(−R/L)) ≈ 3,316 km → f ≈ 22.6 Hz; the area-weighted disc average gives H_eff ≈ 2,140 km → f ≈ 35 Hz. Both are far from 7.83 Hz. Across the entire disc — from pole (8.77 Hz) to rim (89.5 Hz) — no radius yields 7.83 Hz. He resolves the contradiction by abandoning his geometry and using the globe formula.
patches.rationale: Two fixes: (1) Update equatorial height 1,270→1,498 km and frequency 59→50 Hz (arithmetic correction). (2) Replace vague 'averaging pole and equatorial heights' (which gives 14.9 Hz, not 22 Hz) with the explicit linear radial integral H_eff ≈ 3,316 km → 22.6 Hz and the monotonicity observation. All values verified by curmudgeon quantitative check.
